reasoning: As of November 9, 2025, the Federal Reserve has made exactly two rate cuts this year: a 25 bps cut in September and a 25 bps cut in October. Under the market criteria, determining the number of cuts requires adding up 25 bps-equivalent increments. With two cuts completed thus far and only one scheduled FOMC meeting remaining in December, exactly four rate cuts would strictly require a 50 bps cut (equivalent to two 25 bps cuts) at the December meeting. Recent CME FedWatch Tool data from early November indicates approximately a 64% probability of a 25 bps cut (which would result in a total of 3 cuts for the year) and a ~36% probability of a pause in December (resulting in 2 total cuts). The probability of a 50 bps cut is near zero, as monetary policy is leaning more hawkish/cautious. Barring an unforeseen economic emergency prompting an inter-meeting cut or a jumbo December cut, reaching exactly four cuts is practically impossible. Therefore, the probability is set at the minimum bound of 0.02.
